title: Geometric Vectors: Magnitude, Direction, and Equality
methodology: DeepSeek API generation + local validator/renderer QA

lessonAnalysis.coreLearningGoal: Understand the definition of a vector, distinguish between scalar and vector quantities, and identify equal, opposite, and parallel vectors.
successCriteria: Define a vector as having magnitude and direction.
Give examples of scalars and vectors.
Identify equal, opposite, and parallel vectors from a diagram.
lessonExamplesDetected: Comparison of arrows u, v, w on a grid
targetProducts: Identify equal and opposite vectors from a set of arrows.
inputEvidenceStudentsShouldUse: Arrows u, v, w on a grid
doNotRevealAsQuestionCondition: The specific arrows u, v, w and their relationships

problemAnchor: A hiker walks 5 km east, then 5 km north. What is the displacement?

abt.and: You know scalars have only magnitude.
abt.but: Many real-world quantities need direction too.
abt.therefore: We use vectors to describe both magnitude and direction.

objective: By the end of this lesson, you will be able to identify and describe geometric vectors, including equal, opposite, and parallel vectors, and use true bearing notation.

knowledgeNodes.name: Scalar vs Vector
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher: A scalar has magnitude only, like mass or temperature. A vector has magnitude and direction, like displacement or velocity.

knowledgeNodes.name: Vector Notation
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher: Vector AB starts at A and ends at B.

knowledgeNodes.name: Equal Vectors
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher: Equal vectors have the same magnitude and same direction even if they are in different locations.

knowledgeNodes.name: Opposite Vectors
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher: Opposite vectors have the same magnitude but opposite directions.

knowledgeNodes.name: Parallel Vectors
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher: Parallel vectors can point in the same or opposite direction.

knowledgeNodes.name: True Bearing
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher: True bearing is measured clockwise from north. Example: southeast is 135 degrees.

errorTaxonomy.meaning: Misidentifies equal vectors (ignores direction or magnitude)
Misinterprets true bearing (e.g., uses counterclockwise or from east)
Confuses vector notation (e.g., AB vs BA direction)

stemParts.content: Which of the following is a vector quantity?
stemParts.value: Which of the following is a vector quantity?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: Mass, temperature, and time are scalars; they have only magnitude.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: Mass, temperature, and time are scalars; they have only magnitude.

options.label: C
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! Displacement has both magnitude and direction.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: Mass, temperature, and time are scalars; they have only magnitude.

stemParts.content: Vector AB is drawn from A to B. What does vector BA represent?
stemParts.value: Vector AB is drawn from A to B. What does vector BA represent?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: BA starts at B and ends at A, so it points opposite to AB.

options.label: B
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! BA has the same magnitude but opposite direction to AB.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: BA starts at B and ends at A, so it points opposite to AB.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: BA starts at B and ends at A, so it points opposite to AB.

stemParts.content: In Figure F01, explain why vectors u and v are equal even though they start at different points.
stemParts.value: In Figure F01, explain why vectors u and v are equal even though they start at different points.

questions.rubric: Student must state that equal vectors have the same magnitude and direction, and that location does not matter. (1 point for magnitude, 1 point for direction, 1 point for location irrelevance)
questions.sampleStrongAnswer: Vectors u and v are equal because they have the same magnitude (length 3 units) and the same direction (pointing right). The starting point does not affect equality; only magnitude and direction matter.

stemParts.content: A plane flies due west. What is its true bearing?
stemParts.value: A plane flies due west. What is its true bearing?

questions.rubric: Correct answer: 270°. (1 point for understanding bearing from north, 1 point for correct angle)
questions.sampleStrongAnswer: The true bearing of due west is 270°, because true bearing is measured clockwise from north, and west is 270° clockwise from north.

reteachModules.trigger: Student confuses scalar and vector quantities.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Thinks all quantities with numbers are scalars, ignoring direction.

miniLesson.explain: Scalars have only magnitude (size). Vectors have both magnitude and direction. Examples: mass (scalar), weight (vector).
miniLesson.example: Temperature 30°C is scalar; wind 30 km/h north is vector.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Think: 'S' for scalar = single number; 'V' for vector = value + direction.

guidedPractice.prompt: Give an example of a vector quantity and explain why.
guidedPractice.answer: Displacement: 5 km east has magnitude 5 km and direction east.

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Thinks vectors must start at same point to be equal.

miniLesson.explain: Equal vectors have the same magnitude and direction, regardless of starting point. They are translations of each other.
miniLesson.example: Two arrows of length 3 pointing right, one at (0,0) and one at (1,1), are equal.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Equal vectors are like identical twins: same size, same orientation, different location.

guidedPractice.prompt: If vector a has length 2 and points up, and vector b has length 2 and points up, are they equal?
guidedPractice.answer: Yes, if they have same magnitude and direction.

guidedPractice.prompt: If vector a has length 2 and points up, and vector b has length 2 and points down, are they equal?
Draw two equal vectors that start at different points.
guidedPractice.answer: Two arrows of same length and direction, e.g., from (0,0) to (1,0) and from (2,2) to (3,2).

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Thinks opposite vectors are just parallel or have different magnitudes.

miniLesson.explain: Opposite vectors have the same magnitude but exactly opposite directions (180° apart).
miniLesson.example: Vector east 5 km and vector west 5 km are opposites.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Opposite vectors are like mirror images: same size, opposite way.

guidedPractice.prompt: If vector p is 3 units north, what is its opposite?
If vector q is 10 units east, what is its opposite?
Can two vectors be opposite if they have different magnitudes?
guidedPractice.answer: No, they must have the same magnitude.

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Uses counterclockwise or starts from east.

miniLesson.explain: True bearing is measured clockwise from north, from 0° to 360°. North=0°, East=90°, South=180°, West=270°.
miniLesson.example: Southeast is halfway between south (180°) and east (90°), so 135°.

guidedPractice.prompt: What is the true bearing of east?
What is the true bearing of southwest?
A ship sails in the direction of 060°. What compass direction is that?

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Thinks AB and BA are the same vector.

miniLesson.explain: Vector AB starts at A and ends at B. Vector BA starts at B and ends at A. They have the same magnitude but opposite direction.
miniLesson.example: If A=(0,0) and B=(1,0), AB is (1,0) and BA is (-1,0).
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: The first letter is the tail, the second is the head. AB goes from A to B.

guidedPractice.prompt: If vector CD goes from C to D, what is the opposite vector?
If vector PQ has magnitude 5, what is the magnitude of QP?
Explain why AB and BA are not equal.

questions.stem: Give one example of a scalar and one example of a vector.
questions.rubric: Scalar: mass, temperature, etc. Vector: displacement, velocity, etc.

questions.stem: Two vectors are equal if they have the same magnitude and:
Explain why two vectors can be equal even if they start at different points.
questions.rubric: Equal vectors have same magnitude and direction; location does not matter.

questions.stem: If vector a is 4 units north, its opposite is:
What must be true about two vectors for them to be opposite?
What is the true bearing of northeast?
Vector AB is from A to B. Vector BA is:
If vector XY has magnitude 7, what is the magnitude of YX?

teacherEscalation.condition: Student scores 0-4 on diagnostic and reteach modules do not improve understanding.
teacherEscalation.message: Student may have fundamental misconceptions about vectors. Consider one-on-one intervention with visual aids and manipulatives.
